title: Why column lists matter
summary: Learn why specifying column lists in INSERT statements is crucial for safe and predictable data insertion in SQL.

sketchBlocks.title: Why Use Column Lists in INSERT?
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When inserting data into a table, you can write:

```sql
INSERT INTO inventory_items VALUES (7, 'Desk Lamp', 'Decor', 22.50, 'active', 0, NULL, '2026-02-10');
```

But this approach is risky! If the table structure changes (columns are added, removed, or reordered), your statement may break or insert data into the wrong columns.

A safer way is to **explicitly name the columns**:

```sql
INSERT INTO inventory_items (id, name, category, price, status, is_test, notes, last_updated)
VALUES (7, 'Desk Lamp', 'Decor', 22.50, 'active', 0, NULL, '2026-02-10');
```

This makes your intent clear and protects against schema changes. It also helps avoid mistakes with default values and NULLs.

sketchBlocks.title: Defaults and NULLs in INSERTs
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Some columns in `inventory_items` have default values (like `status` and `is_test`). If you omit these columns in your INSERT statement and don't provide values, SQL will use the defaults. For example:

```sql
INSERT INTO inventory_items (id, name, category, price, last_updated)
VALUES (8, 'Mini Plant', 'Decor', 8.00, '2026-02-11');
```

Here, `status` becomes `'draft'` and `is_test` becomes `0` automatically. However, if you omit a column **without a default** (like `name`), SQL will return an error because `name` is `NOT NULL` and has no default.

quizDraft.title: Insert a row without specifying columns
quizDraft.prompt:
Write an INSERT statement that adds a new row to `inventory_items` with these values, **without** specifying a column list:

- id: 9
- name: 'Sample Coaster'
- category: 'Accessories'
- price: 3.50
- status: 'active'
- is_test: 0
- notes: NULL
- last_updated: '2026-02-12'

Use the VALUES clause and do not name the columns.
quizDraft.hint: List all values in the order the columns appear in the table.

help.concept: When you omit the column list in an INSERT, you must provide a value for every column in the table, in the exact order defined by the schema.
help.hint_1: Check the table's column order and match your VALUES to it.
help.hint_2: Start with INSERT INTO inventory_items VALUES (...), filling in all columns in order.

quizDraft.solutionCode: INSERT INTO inventory_items VALUES (9, 'Sample Coaster', 'Accessories', 3.50, 'active', 0, NULL, '2026-02-12');

quizDraft.title: Insert a row with a column list
quizDraft.prompt:
Write an INSERT statement that adds a new row to `inventory_items` with these values, **specifying only the columns you provide**:

- id: 10
- name: 'Test Keychain'
- category: 'Accessories'
- price: 2.00
- last_updated: '2026-02-13'

Let SQL fill in the other columns using their defaults.
quizDraft.hint: List only the columns you have values for in the column list.

help.concept: Specifying a column list lets you insert values only for those columns, and SQL will use default values for any omitted columns that have defaults.
help.hint_1: Include only id, name, category, price, and last_updated in your column list.
help.hint_2: Omitted columns like status and is_test will use their default values.

quizDraft.solutionCode: INSERT INTO inventory_items (id, name, category, price, last_updated) VALUES (10, 'Test Keychain', 'Accessories', 2.00, '2026-02-13');

quizDraft.title: What happens if you omit a required column?
quizDraft.prompt:
Try to write an INSERT statement that omits the `name` column (which is NOT NULL and has no default) for a new row in `inventory_items`. Use these values:

- id: 11
- category: 'Decor'
- price: 6.00
- last_updated: '2026-02-14'

What do you expect will happen?
quizDraft.hint: Omitting a NOT NULL column without a default causes an error.

help.concept: If you omit a required (NOT NULL) column with no default, SQL will not know what value to use and will return an error.
help.hint_1: Try writing the INSERT statement without the name column.
help.hint_2: You should expect an error about a missing NOT NULL value.

quizDraft.solutionCode: INSERT INTO inventory_items (id, category, price, last_updated) VALUES (11, 'Decor', 6.00, '2026-02-14'); -- This will fail because 'name' is required

quizDraft.title: Why is it safer to use column lists in INSERT statements?
quizDraft.prompt: Select ALL reasons why using a column list in an INSERT statement is safer than omitting it.
quizDraft.hint: Think about schema changes, column order, and default values.

help.concept: Column lists make your intent clear, protect against schema changes, and prevent data from being inserted into the wrong columns.
help.hint_1: Column lists help avoid errors if the table structure changes.
help.hint_2: They also make it easier to use default values and avoid mismatches.

options.a: It protects your code if columns are added, removed, or reordered.
options.b: It ensures values go into the correct columns.
options.c: It makes your statement shorter and faster.
options.d: It allows SQL to use default values for omitted columns.
options.e: It is required by SQL syntax.

quizDraft.title: Default values in INSERT statements
quizDraft.prompt: When you omit the `status` column in an INSERT into `inventory_items`, what value will SQL use for `status`?
quizDraft.hint: Check the table's default for the status column.

help.concept: If a column has a DEFAULT value and you omit it in your INSERT, SQL will use that default value.
help.hint_1: Look at the schema for the default value of status.
help.hint_2: The default for status is set in the CREATE TABLE statement.

quizDraft.template: If you omit the status column, SQL will use: ____
